1: לָקַח יַעֲקֹב אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ — "Jacob has taken everything belonging to our father." The verb lāqaḥ (to take) — used here accusatorially; Laban's sons describe Jacob's legitimate wage-earnings as a taking/seizure. וּמֵאֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ עָשָׂה אֵת כָּל-הַכָּבֹד הַזֶּה (and from what belonged to our father he has made all this wealth) — kāḇôḏ (wealth, honor, glory — the root meaning is "heaviness"; wealth is what weighs heavily; cf. 13:2 where Abram is described as "heavy/wealthy" in cattle, silver, and gold). The sons perceive Jacob's prosperity as their loss — a zero-sum view of wealth that is explicitly corrected in v. 9: God, not Jacob, transferred the livestock.
2: פְּנֵי לָבָן (pĕnê lāḇān, the face of Laban) — "Jacob saw that Laban's face was not with him as before." Pānîm (face) is the chapter's primary perceptual-relational indicator: one reads the face of another to determine their attitude, goodwill, or hostility. The "face" idiom runs through vv. 2 and 5: Jacob reads Laban's face and sees the changed relationship. The same face-vocabulary appeared in 32:20 and 33:10 (seeing Jacob's face is like seeing the face of God) and will appear at the Peniel encounter in 32:30. The OT uses "face" as a synecdoche for the entire relational stance of a person.
3: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-יַעֲקֹב שׁוּב אֶל-אֶרֶץ אֲבוֹתֶיךָ — "the LORD said to Jacob: return to the land of your fathers." Šûḇ (to return, turn back) — the verb of covenantal return; the same word used for moral repentance (Isa 55:7; Jer 3:12-14) carries a more literal sense here: physically return to the covenant land. Wĕʾel-môlaḏtĕḵā (and to your birthplace) — the same word as 12:1 where Abraham was told to leave his môlaḏāh; here Jacob is commanded to return to Abraham's môlaḏāh. וְאֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ (and I will be with you) — the divine presence promise of 28:15 ("I am with you and will watch over you") is repeated as the warrant for the return; YHWH's presence is portable, not confined to Bethel or to Canaan.
4: Jacob summons Rachel and Leah to the field — away from Laban's household — to speak privately. The location (field, away from the house) is tactically chosen: Jacob knows the domestic space is surveilled by Laban's household; he chooses the open field where his flocks are, his domain, for the family council. The summoning of both wives together for a joint decision is notable — Jacob treats them as equal partners in the decision to flee, though the decision has already been divinely mandated (v. 3).
5: פְּנֵי אֲבִיכֶן אֵינֶנּוּ אֵלַי כִּתְמֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם — "your father's face is not toward me as yesterday and the day before." The idiom kitmōl šilšôm (as yesterday and the day before) = formerly, previously; the standard Hebrew expression for a prior established state. Jacob reads Laban's face (v. 2) and now articulates it to Rachel and Leah: the relational warmth has ended. וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי הָיָה עִמָּדִי (but the God of my father has been with me) — the contrast: Laban's face has changed, but God's faithfulness has not. The "God of my father" (ʾĔlōhê ʾāḇî) becomes Jacob's standard divine reference throughout this chapter (vv. 5, 29, 42, 53).
6: בְּכָל-כֹּחִי עָבַדְתִּי אֶת-אֲבִיכֶן (bĕḵol-kōḥî ʿāḇaḏtî ʾet-ʾăḇîḵen) — "with all my strength I have served your father." Kōaḥ (strength, power, capacity) — Jacob claims maximal effort: not partial, not adequate, but total commitment. The legal context of this claim will appear in vv. 38-42 where Jacob defends his service record in detail. The witness to Rachel and Leah is important: he is establishing the factual record of his labor before the family audience.
7: וַיַּחֲלֵף אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (wayyaḥălēp ʾet-maśkurtî ʿăśeret mônîm) — "he changed my wages ten times." Māśkōret (wages, hire — from śāḵar, to hire; the payment due to a hired worker; cf. the teraphim-related hire in 30:18 where Leah "hired" Jacob for a night with the mandrakes). ʿĂśeret mônîm (ten times) — a round number expressing complete repetition of wrongdoing (cf. Num 14:22 where Israel tested YHWH "these ten times" — the idiom of maximal accumulative offense). וְלֹא-נְתָנוֹ אֱלֹהִים לְהָרַע עִמָּדִי (but God did not allow him to harm me) — the theological interpretation: Laban's serial wage-changes were overridden by divine protection. Jacob's account gives God the agency in his prosperity, not his own cleverness with the breeding rods (30:37-42).
8: Jacob explains the mechanism of God's overriding of Laban's wage manipulations: whenever Laban specified "speckled as your wages," all the animals bore speckled young; whenever "streaked," all bore streaked. The narrative here revises the ch. 30 account (where Jacob seemed to use selective breeding techniques with rods) by attributing the outcome to divine action. The dream-revelation in vv. 10-12 gives Jacob the divine perspective: God showed him the mechanism. The two accounts are complementary, not contradictory: Jacob used the rods; God overrode the natural odds to produce the contracted pattern.
9: וַיַּצֵּל אֱלֹהִים אֶת-מִקְנֵה אֲבִיכֶם וַיִּתֶּן-לִי — "God has taken away your father's livestock and given it to me." Hiṣṣîl (Hiphil of nāṣal, to deliver, rescue, strip away) — the same verb used for deliverance from slavery and from enemies (Exod 3:8; 18:9-10; Judg 6:9). God "strips away" Laban's livestock and transfers them to Jacob: the language of divine deliverance applied to an economic reallocation. The theological claim is significant: Jacob's wealth is not the result of his skill or Laban's generosity but divine redistribution.
10: בְּעֵת יַחֵם הַצֹּאן (bĕʿēt yaḥem haṣṣōʾn) — "in the breeding season of the flock." Yāḥam (to be in heat, to warm — the verb for sexual arousal/readiness in animals; used for the male goats coming to mate; cf. Jer 2:24 for the same verb). Jacob's dream occurs at the moment of maximum biological activity — the breeding season — which is precisely when the selective breeding of ch. 30 was employed. The dream at the breeding season contextualizes the revelation: God reveals his management of the very moment Jacob thought he was managing.
11: מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים (malʾaḵ hāʾĔlōhîm, angel of God) — the divine messenger speaks to Jacob in the dream. The identification is the same as the messenger who appeared to Hagar (16:7; 21:17), who stopped Abraham at the Akedah (22:11-15), and who will accompany the Exodus (Exod 23:20). In the immediately following divine speech (v. 13), the messenger shifts from third-person divine speech to first-person self-identification ("I am the God of Bethel") — the angel and YHWH blur in the theophanic pattern of Genesis. Hinnēnî — Jacob's response (here I am, ready) — the covenant-readiness formula of Abraham (22:1, 7, 11) and Isaiah (6:8) is now Jacob's.
12: כִּי רָאִיתִי אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לָבָן עֹשֶׂה לָּךְ — "for I have seen all that Laban has been doing to you." Rāʾîtî (I have seen) — the divine seeing that precedes divine action (cf. 29:32 where YHWH "sees" Leah's affliction; 16:13 where Hagar names the God who sees; 31:42 where God has "seen" Jacob's hardship). The divine declaration of having seen is the warrant for the command to return: God has observed the injustice and is now acting to rectify it. The vision reveals the ram patterns as a divine sign, not merely a natural occurrence managed by Jacob's rods.
13: אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית-אֵל (ʾānōḵî hāʾēl bêt-ʾēl) — "I am the God of Bethel." An unusual divine self-identification: YHWH/the angel identifies himself not by covenant relationship ("God of Abraham, God of Isaac") but by a specific place and event — the Bethel theophany of 28:10-22. The identification links back to Jacob's dream at Bethel (the pillar, the oil, the vow) and confirms that the God who met Jacob at that night is the same God now commanding his return. ʾĂšer māšaḥtā šām maṣṣēḇāh (where you anointed the pillar) — the specific act (anointing the pillar with oil, 28:18) is cited as the identifying event. ʾĂšer nāḏartā lî šām neḏer (where you made me a vow there) — the Bethel vow (28:20-22) is now being called in: Jacob vowed; God kept his end (provision, protection, return); now the vow's condition is fulfilled and Jacob must return to the God who met him at Bethel.
14: הַעוֹד לָנוּ חֵלֶק וְנַחֲלָה בְּבֵית אָבִינוּ — "do we still have any share and inheritance in our father's house?" Ḥēleq (portion, share) and naḥălāh (inheritance, inherited possession) — the legal inheritance vocabulary of the OT. Leah and Rachel are raising the question of their own disinheritance: have they been treated as heirs by their father or as commodities? The question is rhetorical — they already know the answer (v. 15: he has treated them as foreigners).
15: הֲלוֹא נָכְרִיּוֹת נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ לוֹ (hălōʾ nāḵriyyôt neḥšaḇnû lô) — "have we not been regarded as foreigners by him?" Nāḵriyyāh (foreign woman, outsider — feminine of nāḵrî, foreign, alien, stranger; same root as hikkîr, to recognize — the outsider is the one not recognized). The daughters of Laban have been treated as non-family by their own father: kî-ḵāhāh môḵerānû wayyōḵal gam-ʾāḵôl ʾet-kasĕpēnû (he has sold us and even used up the bride-price paid for us) — the bride-price (môhar) should have gone to the bride's family and eventually to the bride herself; Laban consumed it, treating his daughters as sources of revenue rather than as family members with inheritance rights.
16: וְעַתָּה כֹּל אֲשֶׁר אָמַר אֱלֹהִים אֵלֶיךָ עֲשֵׂה — "now, whatever God has told you, do it." Rachel and Leah align themselves completely with Jacob's divine mandate. Their endorsement is the family consensus that Jacob needed: the departure is not just divinely commanded but family-sanctioned. The wealth Jacob is taking belongs, they argue, to them and their children anyway (v. 16: "surely all the wealth God took from our father belongs to us and our children") — so there is no theft in the departure. The daughters' theological alignment ("whatever God says") is their equivalent of the covenant-obedience formula.
17: Jacob acts: children and wives on camels, livestock ahead, goods packed. The departure sequence is rapid and comprehensive — the narrative lists the cargo categories to show that nothing is left behind. The movement is urgent: Rachel has already taken the teraphim (v. 19) and the departure must be completed before Laban returns from the sheep-shearing.
18: מִקְנֵהוּ אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ (miqnēhû ʾăšer rāḵaš) — "his livestock that he had acquired." Rāḵaš (to acquire, accumulate — used for the accumulated wealth of Abraham in 12:5 and Esau in 36:6; a word for legally owned acquired property, not inherited). Jacob's departure with his legally acquired livestock parallels Abraham's departure from Egypt with his acquired wealth (12:16; 13:2) — both patriarchs depart enriched by foreign sojourn. Paddān ʾărāmāh (Paddan-Aram) → ʾereṣ kĕnaʿan (land of Canaan): the geographical movement is explicitly stated.
19: וַתִּגְנֹב רָחֵל אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִיהָ (wattiḡnōḇ rāḥel ʾet-hattĕrāpîm ʾăšer lĕʾāḇîhā) — "Rachel stole her father's household gods." Gānaḇ (to steal) — the same verb as Jacob "stealing" Laban's heart (v. 20) and Laban's accusation about the teraphim (v. 30). The verb creates a pattern of stealing: Rachel steals gods, Jacob steals the departure. Tĕrāpîm — household idols used in divination (1 Sam 15:23 links tĕrāpîm to divination; Ezek 21:21; Hos 3:4; Zech 10:2). Their function as potential inheritance-claim objects (Nuzi texts from the 15th-14th c. BCE indicate that possession of the paternal household gods could support an inheritance claim) may explain Rachel's motivation, though the text does not explicitly state it. The teraphim subplot creates narrative tension (Laban searches; Jacob unknowingly vows death on the thief, v. 32; Rachel hides them) that is resolved without resolution — the teraphim are never found or returned.
20: וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת-לֵב לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי (wayyiḡnōḇ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-lēḇ lāḇān hāʾărammî) — "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean." The idiom gānaḇ lēḇ (steal the heart) = to act without someone's knowledge, to deceive someone's mind: "he stole Laban's heart" by not telling him he was leaving. The verbal echo of v. 19 (gānaḇ for both Rachel's physical theft and Jacob's deceptive departure) is deliberate: both theft-acts occur simultaneously; the family's departure is a complex of taking (lāqaḥ) and stealing (gānaḇ). ʿAl-bĕlî higgîḏ lô kî bōrēaḥ hûʾ (by not telling him that he was fleeing) — bāraḥ (to flee, run away) — Jacob's departure is a flight, not a dignified leave-taking.
21: וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת-הַנָּהָר (wayyaʿăḇōr ʾet-hannāhār) — "he crossed the river." ʿĀḇar (to cross over, pass through) — the same verb for the crossing of the Euphrates that Abraham made in reverse at the beginning of the patriarchal narrative (the ʿîḇrî, "Hebrew," is "the one who crossed over"). Jacob crosses the Euphrates going the opposite direction from Abraham — he is returning from the origin-country to the covenant land. Wayyāśem ʾet-pānāyw har haggīlʿāḏ (he set his face toward the hill country of Gilead) — the idiom śûm pānîm (set the face) = to determine, resolve, fix one's direction; cf. Luke 9:51 where Jesus "set his face" toward Jerusalem.
22: בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (bayyôm haššĕlîšî) — "on the third day." The three-day gap between Jacob's departure and Laban's notification repeats the three-day structure of significant intervals in Genesis (22:4 — Abraham reaches Moriah on the third day). Laban learns of the departure three days after the fact — Jacob has a significant head start. The third-day structure here may be purely narrative (a realistic travel interval) or may carry the weight of the pattern of decisive action following a period of delay.
23: וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרָיו דֶּרֶךְ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים — "he pursued him for seven days." Laban pursues with his relatives for seven days — a full week of travel — to catch Jacob in the Gilead hills. The pursuit is overtly hostile: he takes his kinsmen, implying the threat of force. Wayyaḏḇēq ʾōtô bĕhar haggīlʿāḏ (he overtook him in the hill country of Gilead) — dāḇaq (to cling, stick, overtake — the same word for the covenant loyalty of Ruth 1:14 ["Ruth clung to her"]; here it describes hostile pursuit). Laban closes the gap; without the divine intervention of v. 24, the confrontation could be violent.
24: פֶּן-תְּדַבֵּר עִם-יַעֲקֹב מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע (pen-tĕdabbēr ʿim-yaʿăqōḇ miṭṭôḇ ʿaḏ-rāʿ) — "lest you speak to Jacob from good to bad." The polar merism miṭṭôḇ ʿaḏ-rāʿ (from good to bad) = anything at all; a merism that comprehends the entire spectrum between two extremes (cf. 24:50: Laban said he could say "nothing to you good or bad"). The identical prohibition was given (in inverse form) for the treaty in 24:50; here it is a divine restriction on Laban. The warning is absolute: God forbids Laban from using any speech — favorable or unfavorable — to alter Jacob's course. The divine protection of Jacob's return is verbal as well as physical.
25: Laban overtakes Jacob in the Gilead hills; both parties camp. The confrontation is deferred by the overnight camp — the actual confrontation begins in vv. 26-42. The tent-setting (ṭāpā ʾet-ʾōhŏlô) marks territorial establishment: Laban sets up camp in Jacob's territory (the hill country where Jacob has fled). The spatial dynamic: Laban has pursued Jacob into Jacob's chosen refuge; Jacob cannot flee further. The confrontation is forced.
26: מֶה עָשִׂיתָ וַתִּגְנֹב אֶת-לְבָבִי (meh ʿāśîtā wattignōḇ ʾet-lĕḇāḇî) — "what have you done? You have stolen my heart." Laban's accusation uses both the heart-stealing idiom (v. 20) and the captive-metaphor: kišĕḇûyôt ḥāreḇ (like captives taken by the sword). The sword-captive metaphor is hyperbolic — Jacob left peacefully with his family and livestock — but it expresses Laban's sense of violation: his daughters have been taken as if by force, without ceremony or farewell. Lāmāh naḵaḇtā lāḇōraḥ (why did you hide yourself in fleeing?) — naḵāh (to hide oneself, steal away) — Laban's complaint is about the secrecy, not about the departure itself.
27: לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ אֹתִי — "why did you steal away from me?" Laban's complaint about the theft of departure contrasts with what he says he would have done: send Jacob off with joy, song, timbrels (tuppîm), and harps (kinnôr). The festive-farewell vocabulary describes the opposite of a fugitive's midnight flight. Whether Laban would actually have done this (or used the occasion to prevent Jacob's departure) is not resolvable from the text; Jacob himself answers in v. 31 that he was afraid Laban would have taken his daughters by force.
28: לֹא נְטַשְׁתַּנִי לְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנַי וְלִבְנֹתָי — "you did not allow me to kiss my grandchildren and my daughters." Laban's complaint shifts from legal accusation (theft) to relational grief: he was denied the farewell kiss. The kiss of blessing and farewell is a covenantal-relational gesture in the ANE (cf. 29:13 where Laban kissed Jacob at his arrival; 31:55 where he will kiss his grandchildren before departing). Nāšaq (to kiss) in the patriarchal narratives marks recognition, acceptance, and blessing-transfer. Laban claims relational injury as well as legal injury.
29: יֶשׁ-לְאֵל יָדִי לַעֲשׂוֹת עִמָּכֶם רָע (yĕš-lĕʾēl yāḏî laʿăśôt ʿimmāḵem rāʿ) — "it is in my power to do you harm." The phrase lĕʾēl yāḏî (to my hand is El/power) — ʾēl here means "power, ability" rather than the divine name (though the orthography is identical); the idiom yĕš lĕyāḏ = "it is within one's ability/reach"; cf. Deut 28:32; Prov 3:27: "when it is in your power to do good." Laban explicitly acknowledges his restraint is God-imposed: the divine dream-warning (v. 24) is his reason for not using this power. The admission is a back-handed confession: God has overridden Laban's hostile capacity.
30: לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ אֶת-אֱלֹהָי (lāmmāh gānaḇtā ʾet-ʾĕlōhāy) — "why did you steal my gods?" Laban's final question in this speech is about the teraphim. The phrase ʾĕlōhāy (my gods) — Laban uses the possessive; these are his personal household deities, the objects of his religious practice. The irony that Jacob, the YHWH-worshipper, is accused of stealing Laban's gods (which he himself has called "my gods") is sharp: the narrative implicitly contrasts the portable, universal YHWH with Laban's localized household gods, which can be stolen and sat upon (v. 35).
31: כִּי יָרֵאתִי (kî yārēʾtî, because I was afraid) — Jacob's one-word explanation for the secret departure: fear. The admission is honest and unsentimental. Pen-tiḡzōl ʾet-bĕnōtêḵā mēʿimmāḏî (lest you take your daughters from me by force) — gāzal (to seize by force, tear away — a word for violent seizure: Lev 19:13; Mic 2:2; Mal 1:13). Jacob's fear was that Laban would use force to end the marriage; given Laban's track record of deception (the Leah/Rachel substitution, the ten wage-changes), the fear is reasonable. The flight was driven by self-protective fear, not hostility.
32: אֲשֶׁר תִּמְצָא אֶת-אֱלֹהֶיךָ לֹא יִחְיֶה (ʾăšer timṣāʾ ʾet-ʾĕlōhêḵā lōʾ yiḥyeh) — "whoever has your gods shall not live." Jacob's death-curse on the teraphim-thief is uttered without knowing Rachel is the thief. The dramatic irony is complete: the man who unknowingly pronounced a death-curse on his favorite wife will spend years grieving her death in childbirth (35:16-20). Whether the curse has any causal relationship to Rachel's death is theologically unresolved by the narrator; the connection is structural in the narrative. Wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ yaʿăqōḇ kî rāḥēl gĕnāḇātam (Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them) — the narrator's explicit note prevents the reader from blaming Jacob; he acts in good faith.
33: Laban's search: he goes through Jacob's tent, Leah's tent, and the tents of the two female servants (Bilhah and Zilpah) — a systematic, ordered search. The detail that Rachel's tent is searched last (after the others have been cleared) creates narrative suspense: will the teraphim be found? The order also reveals the household tent-arrangement: Jacob, Leah, servants, Rachel — the four household units.
34: וְרָחֵל לָקְחָה אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים — Rachel has placed the teraphim inside the camel-saddle (kār, camel cushion/saddle-bag) and is sitting on them. Wayyĕmaššēš lāḇān ʾet-kol-hāʾōhel wĕlōʾ māṣāʾ (Laban searched the whole tent and did not find them) — the thorough but unsuccessful search. The household gods, objects of Laban's religious practice, are hidden under Rachel in a posture that renders them ritually unclean (menstruation, v. 35); the gods that were Laban's strength are powerless to reveal their location.
35: כִּי-דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים לִי (kî-ḏereḵ nāšîm lî) — "because the way of women is with me." The idiom ḏereḵ nāšîm (the way of women) is a euphemism for menstruation (cf. the only other OT occurrence in this idiom-sense). Rachel claims to be menstruating and therefore unable to rise — a socially and ritually compelling excuse in an ancient Near Eastern context where menstruation involved both social conventions of seclusion and ritual impurity concerns (Lev 15:19-30). The teraphim hidden under a menstruating woman are effectively hidden in a place no searching hand would go. Rachel's deception of her father mirrors Jacob's deception of his father in ch. 27 — the family talent for strategic misdirection is shared.
36: וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב וַיָּרֶב בְּלָבָן (wayyiḥar lĕyaʿăqōḇ wayyāreḇ bĕlāḇān) — "Jacob was angry and argued with Laban." Ḥārāh (to burn, be hot — the idiom of anger: ḥārāh ʾaph, the anger burned); the anger here burns in Jacob after the failed search proves he has nothing to hide. Rîḇ (to contend, argue — the word for legal dispute: the same as the covenant dispute in Mic 6:1-2 "YHWH has a dispute [rîb] with his people"). Jacob's defense is a legal speech (vv. 36-42) that would be at home in a legal covenant dispute context: "what is my crime? What have I wronged you with?"
37: שִׂים כֹּה נֶגֶד אַחַי וְאַחֶיךָ וְיוֹכִיחוּ בֵּין שְׁנֵינוּ — "put it here before my relatives and yours, and let them judge between the two of us." Jacob proposes public adjudication: lay out everything before the assembled relatives (Jacob's and Laban's) and let them serve as judges. The word yôḵîḥû (Hiphil of yāḵaḥ — to judge, adjudicate, decide) — the same root as the sign-prayer word in 24:14 (the servant asked God to hôḵîaḥ, designate, the right woman). Jacob's confident invitation to judgment expresses his consciousness of innocence on the theft charge.
38: עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ (ʿeśrîm šānāh ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḵ) — "twenty years I have been with you." Jacob's formal defense begins with the chronological frame: twenty years (14 for the two daughters, 6 for the livestock, v. 41). רְחֵלֶיךָ וְעִזֶּיךָ לֹא שִׁכֵּלוּ (your ewes and female goats have not miscarried) — šikkēl (Piel of šāḵal, to be bereaved of children; in animals, to miscarry — the same root as the child-bereavement of 27:45 and Isa 49:21). The claim is that under Jacob's shepherding, the flocks reproduced perfectly: no miscarriages, no excess predation.
39: טְרֵפָה לֹא-הֵבֵאתִי אֵלֶיךָ אָנֹכִי אֲחַטֶּנָּה — "I did not bring you what was torn by beasts; I bore the loss myself." The legal background: Exod 22:12-13 (later codified) specifies that if an animal in a shepherd's care is killed by a predator, the shepherd is released from liability if he shows the carcass; but if the animal is stolen, the shepherd must make restitution. Jacob claims to have gone beyond legal obligation: he covered even the losses from predation himself, absorbing costs that legally should have been Laban's. מִיָּדִי תְּבַקְשֶׁנָּה גְּנֻבְתִי יוֹם וּגְנֻבְתִי לָיְלָה (you held me responsible for whatever was stolen by day or night) — Jacob worked under a stricter standard than the law required.
40: הָיִיתִי בַיּוֹם אֲכָלַנִי חֹרֶב (hāyîtî ḇayyôm ʾăḵālanî ḥōreḇ) — "by day the heat devoured me." Ḥōreḇ (scorching heat, drought — the same word as the sacred mountain Horeb; the dual meaning of the dry wilderness and the heat of the day). Qeraḥ ballāylāh (cold at night) — the temperature extremes of pastoral life in the Syro-Palestinian uplands: intense daytime heat and cold nights. וַתִּדַּד שְׁנָתִי מֵעֵינָי (sleep fled from my eyes) — nāḏaḏ (to flee, wander away — used for birds fleeing a nest, Prov 27:8; for fugitives, Isa 10:31; 21:14); here sleep is personified as something that has fled. The image of sleepless, temperature-abused labor is vivid and serves Jacob's legal argument.
41: זֶה-לִּי עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה בְּבֵיתֶךָ — "these twenty years of mine in your house." The second statement of the twenty-year frame; Jacob now itemizes: fourteen years for the two daughters, six years for the flocks. וַתַּחֲלֵף אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (and you changed my wages ten times) — the same phrase as v. 7; the repetition in the formal defense speech creates the rhetorical structure of a legal indictment: the wrong was repeated systematically, not once or accidentally. The twenty-year period and ten wage-changes form the factual core of Jacob's case against Laban.
42: לוּלֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וּפַחַד יִצְחָק הָיָה לִי — "if the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Paḥad of Isaac, had not been with me." Paḥad yiṣḥāq (the Fear/Dread of Isaac, or the God whom Isaac fears) — one of the most unusual divine epithets in the OT; it appears only here and in v. 53. Paḥad (fear, dread, terror — the awesome reverence before YHWH; cf. Job 4:14; Ps 119:120; Isa 2:10, 19, 21 where God's presence produces this paḥad). The epithet names God from Isaac's subjective experience of theophanic encounter: the God Isaac knows through fear/awe at the revelations of his life (26:24-25; the Akedah's aftermath). כִּי-רָאָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עָנְיִי (because God has seen my affliction) — ʿŏnî (affliction, poverty, hardship) — the word used for Israel's bondage in Egypt (Exod 3:7: "I have seen the affliction of my people"). Wayyôḵaḥ (and he rebuked) — God's rebuke of Laban through the dream-warning was the decisive intervention that neutralized Laban's power.
43: הַבָּנוֹת בְּנֹתַי וְהַבָּנִים בָּנַי — "the daughters are my daughters, the children are my children." Laban's response to Jacob's defense is not a legal counter-argument but a sweeping ownership claim: everything Jacob has is mine (kōl ʾăšer-ʾattāh rōʾeh lî-hûʾ, all that you see is mine). The claim is technically true from Laban's patriarchal perspective — daughters and their offspring belonged to the father's household. וְלִבְנֹתַי מָה-אֶעֱשֶׂה לָאֵלֶּה (what can I do today about my daughters?) — Laban shifts from possession-claim to helplessness; the rhetorical move acknowledges that the situation is irreversible. His verbal dominance (everything is mine) is immediately qualified by practical inability to change anything.
44: וְעַתָּה לְכָה נִכְרְתָה בְרִית אֲנִי וָאָתָּה — "now come, let us cut a covenant, you and I." Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) — the standard covenant-making formula (cf. 15:18; 21:27, 32; 26:28). Having failed to recover the teraphim and having been confronted by Jacob's effective defense, Laban proposes the only remaining option: a covenant that establishes a formal, enforceable boundary. The covenant is Laban's face-saving move: he cannot take his daughters back by force (v. 29: God has forbidden it), he cannot prove the teraphim theft (v. 34-35), and Jacob's labor defense is airtight (vv. 38-42); the covenant allows Laban to exit the confrontation with a structure he can live with.
45: וַיִּקַּח יַעֲקֹב אֶבֶן וַיְרִימֶהָ מַצֵּבָה — "Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar." The standing stone (maṣṣēḇāh) — the same object Jacob erected at Bethel (28:18) for a different purpose: the Bethel pillar was a cultic memorial to theophany; this Galeed pillar is a legal boundary marker for a treaty. The stone serves as both witness and monument: gal (stone heap) + ʿēḏ (witness) = Galeed.
46: Jacob instructs his relatives to gather stones; they pile a heap. The heap is built by communal effort — different from the single standing stone Jacob set up (v. 45); the heap requires multiple people, making it a communal/collective monument. They eat beside the heap — the covenant meal that ratifies the treaty (cf. 26:30 where Isaac's covenant with Abimelech was ratified by eating and drinking; Exod 24:11 where the elders "ate and drank" before YHWH at Sinai).
47: יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא (yĕḡar śāhăḏûtāʾ, Jegar Sahadutha) — Laban's name in Aramaic: yĕḡar = heap (Aramaic; cf. Hebrew gal) + śāhăḏûtāʾ = of witness (Aramaic; cognate to Hebrew ʿēḏût). גַּלְעֵד (galʿēḏ, Galeed) — Jacob's Hebrew name: gal (heap) + ʿēḏ (witness). This is the only bilingual place-name episode in the Torah — the same place receives both an Aramaic and a Hebrew name that mean the same thing. The bilingualism marks the treaty boundary not just geographically but linguistically: Laban speaks Aramaic, Jacob speaks Hebrew; the two languages are the two cultures whose boundary this heap marks.
48: הַגַּל הַזֶּה עֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ הַיּוֹם — "this heap is a witness between me and you today." The heap is formally invested with legal witness-status — it will "see" future violations of the treaty. The use of a material object as a covenant witness is common in the OT (cf. Josh 24:27 where Joshua uses a stone as a witness; Isa 55:4 where David is named as a witness for the nations). ʿAl-kēn qārāʾ-šĕmô galʿēḏ — the Galeed name-explanation is repeated, anchoring it to the covenant-witness function.
49: הַמִּצְפָּה אֲשֶׁר אָמַר יִצֶף יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ — "and Mizpah, because he said: may YHWH watch between me and you." Miṣpāh (watchtower, lookout — from ṣāpāh, to look out, watch; ṣōpeh = watchman, sentinel). The place is named from the function YHWH is asked to perform: to watch (yiṣep = Qal imperfect of ṣāpāh) between the two parties when they are absent from each other. The Mizpah formula ("may YHWH watch between us") has become a Christian farewell blessing; in its original context it is a covenant-enforcement mechanism: YHWH is the unseen witness who oversees the treaty when no human witness is present. The connotation is not merely friendly watching but surveillance of potential violations.
50: The covenant's specific terms: no mistreatment of Laban's daughters and no additional wives. אִם-תְּעַנֶּה אֶת-בְּנֹתַי (if you mistreat my daughters) — ʿānāh (to afflict, oppress, humble) — the covenant protects Leah and Rachel from physical or domestic abuse. אִם-תִּקַּח נָשִׁים עַל-בְּנֹתַי (if you take wives besides my daughters) — the monogamy/family-limitation clause; Jacob already has four women (Leah, Rachel, Bilhah, Zilpah), but Laban forbids any additions. ʾĒn ʾîš ʿimmānû rĕʾēh ʾĔlōhîm ʿēḏ bênî ûḇênêḵā (though no one is with us, remember God is witness between me and you) — the appeal to divine witness precisely when no human witness is present: the covenant is operative beyond the reach of human surveillance.
51: Laban's formal statement of the boundary markers: the heap and the pillar. The heap was built communally (v. 46); the pillar was set by Jacob (v. 45). Both serve as witnesses; both are named in the treaty declaration. The formal enumeration of the witness objects matches ANE treaty-witness conventions where all witness parties (gods, natural objects, mountains) are named.
52: עֵד הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְעֵדָה הַמַּצֵּבָה — "this heap is a witness and the pillar is a witness." The two-witness formula; in Israelite law two witnesses were required for legal validity (Deut 17:6; 19:15). The heap and the pillar together constitute the required pair of treaty witnesses. ʾAm-lōʾ-ʾeʿĕḇōr... lĕrāʿāh (I will not cross... to harm you) — the non-crossing-for-harm formula is mutual: both Laban and Jacob are bound not to cross the boundary with hostile intent. The treaty establishes a permanent territorial separation between the two family-nations.
53: אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי נָחוֹר יִשְׁפְּטוּ בֵינֵינוּ — "may the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us." The invocation uses a plural verb (yišpĕṭû, let them judge) with two divine referents — the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor — apparently treating them as separate divine entities (or as a plural of majesty/emphasis). Jacob's response corrects this: וַיִּשָּׁבַע יַעֲקֹב בְּפַחַד אָבִיו יִצְחָק (Jacob swore by the Paḥad of his father Isaac) — Jacob does not swear by the syncretic pairing Laban invokes but specifically by the God of his own covenant line, the Paḥad (Fear) of Isaac. The distinction marks a theological boundary: Laban's God of Nahor is not identified with YHWH; Jacob's oath is to YHWH alone, identified through the Isaac-epithet.
54: וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב זֶבַח בָּהָר (wayyizbaḥ yaʿăqōḇ zebaḥ bāhār) — "Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain." Zebaḥ (sacrificial meal-offering — a sacrifice of which the worshippers participate; the animal is slaughtered (zāḇaḥ) and then eaten communally; cf. the "peace offering" [šĕlāmîm] structure of Lev 3 where the meat is shared between altar, priests, and worshippers). The covenant is ratified through sacrifice and communal eating — the same pattern as 26:30 (Isaac-Abimelech covenant) and Exod 24:11 (Sinai covenant). They eat and spend the night.
55: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם לָבָן בַּבֹּקֶר וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנָיו וְלִבְנֹתָיו — "Laban rose early in the morning and kissed his grandchildren and daughters." The early morning rising (hiškîm, to rise at dawn — as Abraham in 22:3; 20:8) is here Laban's, not Jacob's: he wakes before the departure to kiss and bless (wayyĕḇāreḵ) his family. The final gesture is relational, not legal — blessing his daughters and grandchildren. Wayyāšob limqōmô (he returned to his place) — the covenant executed, Laban returns; the separation is permanent. The narrator closes the Laban episode without sentimentality: he went home. The twenty-years of relationship end with a morning blessing and a boundary heap.
